what: Travel-time service areas from a facility
title: Accessibility analysis
text: Suppose clinic sits near centre. isochrones nested areas reachable within time budget:
code: clinic <- ox_nearest_nodes(g, x = -34.8553, y = -8.0089) iso <- ox_isochrone(g, clinic, cutoffs = c(60, 120, 180), weight = "travel_time") iso[c("cutoff", "n_nodes")] #> Simple feature collection with 3 features and 2 fields #> Geometry type: POLYGON #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: -34.86427 ymin: -8.017667 xmax: -34.84766 ymax: -7.999988 #> Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84 #>   cutoff n_nodes                       geometry #> 1    180     468 POLYGON ((-34.86237 -8.0066... #> 2    120     368 POLYGON ((-34.86141 -8.0064... #> 3     60     104 POLYGON ((-34.85679 -8.0048... plot(g, col = "grey85", lwd = 0.6) plot(sf::st_geometry(iso), add = TRUE, border = NA,      col = grDevices::adjustcolor(c("#0d3b66", "#1f7a8c", "#2a9d8f"), 0.35))

title: Accessibility analysis
text: several facilities several demand points (homes), distance matrix gives travel time every home every facility; row minimum home’s nearest facility:
code: homes <- ox_nearest_nodes(g,   x = c(-34.8505, -34.852, -34.8585),   y = c(-8.0125, -8.006, -8.011)) facilities <- ox_nearest_nodes(g,   x = c(-34.8553, -34.8500),   y = c(-8.0089, -8.0140))  m <- ox_distance_matrix(g, from = homes, to = facilities, weight = "travel_time") round(m)                 # seconds, homes x facilities #>            8945592343 5515883370 #> 1206764895         76         38 #> 1499391454         73        164 #> 7272198752         95        176 round(apply(m, 1, min))  # travel time to the nearest facility #> 1206764895 1499391454 7272198752  #>         38         73         95

title: Accessibility analysis
text: network access, facilities come straight OpenStreetMap via features module, workflow identical: Overlay population polygons can quantify many people fall outside 20-minute reach — core question accessibility territorial planning.
code: g <- ox_graph_from_place("Olinda, Brazil", network_type = "drive") |>   ox_simplify() |>   ox_add_edge_travel_times()  hospitals <- ox_features_from_place("Olinda, Brazil", tags = list(amenity = "hospital")) xy <- sf::st_coordinates(hospitals) origins <- ox_nearest_nodes(g, xy[, 1], xy[, 2])  # 10- and 20-minute drive-time service areas across all hospitals ox_isochrone(g, origins, cutoffs = c(600, 1200), weight = "travel_time")

title: Features and points of interest
text: Features selected tags, given named list. entry either TRUE (key value) character vector allowed values:
code: # schools in a place ox_features_from_place("Olinda, Brazil", tags = list(amenity = "school"))  # the amenities studied in accessibility research, in one call ox_features_from_place(   "Recife, Brazil",   tags = list(amenity = c("school", "hospital", "pharmacy", "marketplace")) )  # every building footprint (key present, any value) ox_features_from_place("Olinda, Brazil", tags = list(building = TRUE))  # parks and green space ox_features_from_place("Recife, Brazil", tags = list(leisure = "park"))  # public transit stops ox_features_from_place("Recife, Brazil", tags = list(public_transport = "stop_position"))

title: Figure-ground diagrams
text: ox_plot_figure_ground() renders osm_graph. bundled real network central Olinda, Brazil (loaded offline):

title: Figure-ground diagrams
text: package bundles three real networks span morphological spectrum: rigid grid (Midtown Manhattan), irregular colonial core (Olinda), ancient organic city (Rome). Side side, differences immediate:  visual counterpart street-orientation entropy (see Street orientation article): Manhattan’s grid reads crisp directions, Rome’s tangle points everywhere.
code: op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3)) ox_plot_figure_ground(ox_example("manhattan"), title = "Manhattan") ox_plot_figure_ground(ox_example("olinda"),    title = "Olinda") ox_plot_figure_ground(ox_example("rome"),      title = "Rome") par(op)

title: Figure-ground diagrams
text: network access, build figure-ground place. Cropping buffer distance keeps comparison fair: Use ox_graph_from_point() fixed dist (e.g. 805 m ≈ half mile) crop every city one-square-mile window.
code: places <- c("Barcelona, Spain", "Tunis, Tunisia", "Salt Lake City, Utah, USA") op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3)) for (p in places) {   g <- ox_graph_from_address(p, dist = 800, network_type = "drive") |>     ox_simplify()   ox_plot_figure_ground(g, title = p) } par(op)

title: Getting started with osmnxr
text: osmnxr OSMnx R: downloads, models, analyzes visualizes street networks OpenStreetMap. public API tidyverse-friendly returns sf objects; heavy graph computation (routing, metrics, simplification) runs bundled Rust core. central object osm_graph: pair sf tables (nodes edges) plus metadata.

title: Getting started with osmnxr
text: usual entry point place name, downloads OpenStreetMap: vignette runs offline, load exact network — historic centre Olinda, Brazil — copy bundled package:
code: g <- ox_graph_from_place("Olinda, Brazil", network_type = "drive") g <- ox_simplify(g) g <- ox_example("olinda") g #>  #> ── osm_graph ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> 498 nodes, 1191 edges #> Network type: "unknown" #> Simplified: FALSE #> CRS: "WGS 84" plot(g)

title: Getting started with osmnxr
code: ox_basic_stats(g) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   n_nodes n_edges total_length mean_length mean_out_degree self_loops circuity #>     <int>   <int>        <dbl>       <dbl>           <dbl>      <int>    <dbl> #> 1     498    1191       95484.        80.2            2.39          1     1.06

title: Getting started with osmnxr
text: Snap coordinates graph nodes, compute shortest path (Dijkstra, Rust):
code: orig <- ox_nearest_nodes(g, x = -34.8553, y = -8.0089) dest <- ox_nearest_nodes(g, x = -34.8505, y = -8.0125) route <- ox_shortest_path(g, orig, dest) length(route) # nodes along the route #> [1] 8 route_xy <- sf::st_coordinates(g$nodes)[match(route, g$nodes$osmid), ] plot(g, col = "grey80", lwd = 0.6) lines(route_xy, col = "#b7410e", lwd = 3)

code: orig <- ox_nearest_nodes(g, x = -34.8553, y = -8.0089) dest <- ox_nearest_nodes(g, x = -34.8505, y = -8.0125) route <- ox_shortest_path(g, orig, dest, weight = "length") length(route) # nodes along the route #> [1] 8 route_xy <- sf::st_coordinates(g$nodes)[match(route, g$nodes$osmid), ] plot(g, col = "grey80", lwd = 0.6) lines(route_xy, col = "#b7410e", lwd = 3) points(route_xy[c(1, nrow(route_xy)), ], pch = 19, col = "#0d3b66", cex = 1.2)

title: Routing and isochrones
text: Real routing usually minimises time, distance. Impute free-flow speeds road’s class, derive per-edge travel times, route (Boeing 2025, routing module):
code: g <- ox_add_edge_travel_times(g) head(g$edges[c("highway", "length", "speed_kph", "travel_time")]) #> Simple feature collection with 6 features and 4 fields #> Geometry type: LINESTRING #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: -34.85889 ymin: -8.00763 xmax: -34.85675 ymax: -8.006154 #> Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84 #>       highway    length speed_kph travel_time                       geometry #> 1       trunk 143.05987        80    6.437694 LINESTRING (-34.85776 -8.00... #> 2       trunk  39.20625        80    1.764281 LINESTRING (-34.85716 -8.00... #> 3 residential 108.81964        30   13.058356 LINESTRING (-34.85716 -8.00... #> 4 residential 108.81964        30   13.058356 LINESTRING (-34.85675 -8.00... #> 5    tertiary  47.55902        40    4.280311 LINESTRING (-34.85867 -8.00... #> 6    tertiary  47.55902        40    4.280311 LINESTRING (-34.85889 -8.00...  route_t <- ox_shortest_path(g, orig, dest, weight = "travel_time") identical(route_t, route) # may differ: the fastest route is not always shortest #> [1] TRUE

title: Routing and isochrones
text: ox_k_shortest_paths() returns k shortest loopless routes (Yen’s algorithm) — useful comparing options modelling redundancy:
code: ox_k_shortest_paths(g, orig, dest, k = 3, weight = "travel_time") #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>    rank  cost path       #>   <int> <dbl> <list>     #> 1     1  72.1 <dbl [8]>  #> 2     2  90.7 <dbl [11]> #> 3     3 111.  <dbl [16]>

title: Street orientation
text: ox_example("olinda") loads small real network (historic centre Olinda, Brazil) bundled package, runs without network access:  Olinda’s colonial street pattern irregular, rose plot points many directions entropy high.

title: Street orientation
text: package bundles bearings three cities span spectrum — comparison Figure 2 Boeing (2025). real bearings, sampled city’s drivable network: Chicago’s relentless grid gives lowest entropy; New Orleans, bending along Mississippi, sits middle; Rome’s ancient organic core highest — near theoretical maximum log(36) = 3.58.
code: cities <- readRDS(system.file("extdata", "city_orientations.rds", package = "osmnxr")) ent <- tapply(cities$bearing, cities$city, ox_orientation_entropy) round(ent, 3) #>     Chicago New Orleans        Rome  #>       2.473       3.272       3.550 library(ggplot2)  bins <- 36; bw <- 360 / bins cities$sector <- (floor((cities$bearing %% 360) / bw) + 0.5) * bw counts <- as.data.frame(table(city = cities$city, sector = cities$sector)) counts$sector <- as.numeric(as.character(counts$sector))  ggplot(counts, aes(sector, Freq)) +   geom_col(width = bw, fill = "#0d3b66", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +   coord_polar(start = 0) +   scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 360), breaks = seq(0, 315, 45),                      labels = c("N", "NE", "E", "SE", "S", "SW", "W", "NW")) +   facet_wrap(~ city) +   labs(x = NULL, y = NULL,        title = "Street orientation: ordered (Chicago) to organic (Rome)") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +   theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: Urban metrics
text: pieces summary standard urban indicators: n_nodes / n_edges — intersections street segments. Intersection density (nodes per km²) common measure network “grain”. mean_length — average street segment length, proxy block size. total_length — total street length; divide area street density. circuity — much streets deviate straight lines.
code: ox_basic_stats(g) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   n_nodes n_edges total_length mean_length mean_out_degree self_loops circuity #>     <int>   <int>        <dbl>       <dbl>           <dbl>      <int>    <dbl> #> 1     498    1191       95484.        80.2            2.39          1     1.06 area_km2 <- as.numeric(sf::st_area(sf::st_convex_hull(sf::st_union(g$nodes)))) / 1e6 n_intersections <- sum(g$nodes$osmid %in% c(g$edges$u, g$edges$v)) n_intersections / area_km2 # intersections per km^2 #> [1] 151.0356

title: Urban metrics
text: Circuity total street length straight-line distance segment endpoints. value near 1 means straight streets; higher means winding:

title: Urban metrics
text: Betweenness centrality counts share shortest paths passing node. maximum highlights structural chokepoints — “bridge connecting city’s halves” (Boeing & Ha 2024) — single points failure mobility resilience. Map : darkest, largest nodes carry -traffic.  high-betweenness nodes trace -routes hold network together — exactly junctions planner protect reinforce.
code: ct <- ox_centrality(g, type = "betweenness", normalized = TRUE) ct[order(-ct$betweenness), ][1:5, ] #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #>        osmid betweenness #>        <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 6146825103       0.183 #> 2 5662175659       0.178 #> 3 8291701581       0.165 #> 4 1572677068       0.163 #> 5 5662175651       0.160 nodes <- g$nodes nodes$betweenness <- ct$betweenness[match(nodes$osmid, ct$osmid)] plot(g, col = "grey80", lwd = 0.6) plot(nodes["betweenness"], pch = 19,      cex = 0.4 + 4 * nodes$betweenness / max(nodes$betweenness),      pal = function(n) hcl.colors(n, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE), add = TRUE)

osmnxr 0.1.0
text: First public release OSMnx R. ox_example() loads small bundled real network (central Olinda, Brazil) examples vignettes show real analyses offline. vignettes reworked around real cases Boeing (2025) OSMnx examples gallery: multi-city orientation entropy (Chicago/New Orleans/Rome), betweenness chokepoints, travel-time routing isochrones, amenity accessibility. ox_orientation_entropy() ox_plot_orientation() now accept either osm_graph numeric vector bearings. ox_plot_figure_ground() draws figure-ground diagrams, ox_example() now also bundles "manhattan" "rome" networks new “Figure-ground diagrams” vignette (Boeing 2025, Fig. 3). ox_simplify() now preserves traversability per direction, emitting bidirectional edges two-way streets (fixes zero-valued routing/centrality simplified graphs). Download street networks OpenStreetMap (ox_graph_from_place(), ox_graph_from_address(), ox_graph_from_point(), ox_graph_from_bbox()) via Overpass API, returning tidy sf-backed osm_graph object. Geocoding Nominatim (ox_geocode(), ox_geocode_to_sf()). Rust compute core (via extendr): CSR graph, Dijkstra routing (ox_shortest_path(), ox_distances(), ox_nearest_nodes()) metrics (ox_basic_stats(), ox_bearings(), ox_orientation_entropy()). example_osm_graph() synthetic grid offline examples tests. Configuration via ox_settings() session caching ox_clear_cache(). Topology cleanup (Rust core): ox_simplify() merges chains interstitial degree-2 nodes single edges; ox_consolidate_intersections() merges nearby nodes one (via connected components); ox_nearest_edges() finds closest edge point. Advanced routing (Rust core): ox_k_shortest_paths() (Yen’s algorithm), ox_distance_matrix() (many--many), ox_isochrone() (reachable-area polygons via multi-source Dijkstra + concave hull). Travel-time routing: ox_add_edge_speeds() ox_add_edge_travel_times() add speed_kph / travel_time edge columns time-weighted shortest paths isochrones. New vignette: “Routing isochrones”. Network metrics (Rust core): ox_centrality() (betweenness via Brandes’ algorithm, closeness) ox_circuity() (street straightness). Circuity now also reported ox_basic_stats(). Interoperability: ox_as_sfnetwork(), ox_as_tidygraph() ox_as_dodgr() hand graph wider R network ecosystem. Export persistence: ox_to_geojson(), ox_to_maplibre() (style fragment), ox_save_graphml() / ox_load_graphml() lossless round-trip (edge geometry stored WKT), compatible OSMnx / NetworkX / Gephi. Feature downloads: ox_features_from_place() ox_features_from_bbox() fetch POIs, buildings amenities OpenStreetMap tidy sf points. ox_plot_orientation() draws polar rose plot street bearings (ggplot2). Documentation: vignettes “Urban metrics”, “Street orientation”, “Features points interest”, “Interoperability export” “Accessibility analysis”; full pkgdown reference articles menu.
